text: The surgeon retracts the prostate anteriorly, visible at the top of the screen, with the bladder neck behind and the rectum posteriorly. An incision is made to develop a plane just anterior to Denonvilliers' fascia, using sharp and blunt dissection. The midline view shows the creation of a plane posterior to the prostate, with the anterior leaflet of Denonvilliers' fascia and the anterior rectal wall visible behind the suction instrument.

The surgeon uses the da Vinci XI robotic system to rotate the thirty-degree scope upwards, providing a clear view of the posterior surface of the prostate. Sharp dissection is initially used to develop the plane, followed by blunt dissection to gradually peel away Denonvier's fascia. The dissection progresses laterally to release the neurovascular bundles from the posterior side, working on both the patient's left and right sides, moving towards the midline and approaching the posterior aspect of the urethra.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to divide recalcitrant tissue strands while continuing the dissection in a caudal and lateral direction, reaching the curve of the prostatic contour. The neurovascular bundle on the right side is released, and the urethra beyond the apex of the prostate comes into view. The dissection progresses slowly, peeling away Denonvier's fascia to maintain intact nerve supply bilaterally. The prostate contour is visible, confirming oncological clearance. The surgeon uses a Johan's grasper and the fourth arm, Progress, to control the right seminal vesicle and vas, lifting the prostate out of the pelvis.

The surgeon creates space by squeezing the pedicle to thin it out, highlighting the area for the assistant. A haemlock clip is placed, and traction is applied to elevate the prostate, revealing the plane between the neurovascular bundle and the base of the prostate. The clip is placed on the pedicle, allowing the surgeon to tease away the neurovascular bundle in an antegrade fashion. The left hand retracts the prostate towards the midline while the right hand gently teases away the neurovascular bundle, preserving capsular arterial branches as the dissection progresses towards the apex. The neurovascular bundle on the right side is bulky and well-preserved.

The surgeon mobilizes the neurovascular bundle towards the apex of the prostate on the right side, then shifts focus to the left side. The prostate is retracted and elevated using the progress instrument, grasping the left seminal vesicle and vas. The posterior aspect of the prostate is visualized and elevated with the right hand using monopolar sutures. The area for the next Hemolock clip is identified, allowing proximal control of the vascular pedicle on the left side. The pedicle is controlled in an antegrade fashion, and further elevation is achieved with each clip application. Direct vision of the posterior contour of the prostate aids in identifying the pedicle and neurovascular bundle, which becomes visible upon releasing the pedicle.

The surgeon uses spot bipolar diathermy to control a small capsular branch as the dissection progresses towards the mid-prostatic area. Counter traction is applied using a Johan retractor, revealing the basal contour of the prostate on the left side. The neurovascular bundle is protected and retracted with a bipolar grasper, while cold scissors divide the anterior reflection of the lateral prostatic fascia. The incision continues towards the left puboprostatic ligament, preserving the neurovascular bundle and allowing clear visualization of the apex contour for subsequent dissection.

The surgeon releases the nerve on the left-hand side and secures the anterior aspect of the prostate using the progress instrument. On the right-hand side, the anterior aspect of the lateral prostatic fascia is released, and the neurovascular bundle is retracted using the assistant's sucker. A large vein is visible at the top of the high lateral prostatic fascia release, preserved and reflected with the neurovascular bundle. The DVC is divided at the twelve o'clock position using cautery, proceeding towards the apex of the prostate and the urethra in a controlled, stepwise manner.

The surgeon completes the division of the dorsal venous complex (DVC) and exposes the urethra, ensuring hemostatic control with a previously placed suture. The neurovascular bundles are preserved bilaterally, and a catheter is visible as it is withdrawn through the urethra. The posterior aspect of the urethra is divided, and the apex of the prostate is dissected free, providing a clear view of the preserved neurovascular bundles.

I hope you've enjoyed this video.
